1.synthesis: Jesus calls the twelve together and gives them power and authority over all demons and to cure diseases; he sends them out to proclaim the kingdom of God and to heal (vv.1–2). They are to take nothing for the journey — no staff, bag, bread, money, or extra tunic; whatever house they enter, stay there and depart from there; where they are not welcomed, shake the dust from their feet as a testimony against them (vv.3–5). They depart and go through the villages preaching the gospel and healing everywhere (v.6). Meanwhile Herod the tetrarch is perplexed by all that is happening; he hears reports that John has risen, or that Elijah has appeared, or that one of the old prophets has risen. He tries to see Jesus (vv.7–9).
1.historical_context: The mission of the Twelve is a formal commissioning with delegated authority — not merely instruction to assist Jesus but authorization to do what he does (proclaim kingdom, cast out demons, heal). The travel restrictions (no staff, bag, bread, money, extra tunic) are more stringent than the sending of the seventy-two (ch. 10), possibly reflecting the Twelve's special symbolic role as Israel's tribal representatives. The practice of shaking dust from the feet was a Jewish act of disassociation from Gentile contamination when leaving Gentile territory; applied here to unresponsive Jewish towns, it is a dramatic reversal: the unresponsive town is treated as ritually foreign. Herod's perplexity (v.7) parallels the crowd's question of 8:25 ("who is this?") at the level of political authority; the tetrarch who imprisoned John now tries to see the one John prepared for.
1.literary_structure: Luke places the mission of the Twelve immediately before the Feeding of the Five Thousand and Peter's Confession — the three events form a sequence: mission sent out → gathered back and ministry escalation → christological disclosure. The Herod interlude (vv.7–9) functions as a narrative hinge: it introduces the identity-question that Peter's Confession will answer, and it foreshadows the Passion (Herod will reappear at the trial, 23:7–12). The mission's brevity in Luke (vs. Matthew's extended instructions in ch. 10) keeps attention on its essentials: authority given, kingdom proclaimed, healing done.
1.theological_application: "Take nothing for the journey" (v.3). The mission's radical dependence is a theological discipline: the disciples are sent without provisions to force reliance on hospitality received and on the God who commissioned the mission. This is not poverty as virtue but availability as posture — traveling light means being fully available for the kingdom's work rather than managing possessions. The same principle governs Christian mission broadly: those who proclaim the kingdom demonstrate its reality by their dependency on the kingdom's provisions rather than their own preparation.
1.christology: Jesus gives the Twelve power (dynamin) and authority (exousian) — the two dimensions of his own ministry, now delegated. In Luke's Gospel, Jesus is himself characterized by the same power (4:14, 36) and authority (4:32, 36) the Twelve receive; the mission extends his presence and action through them into the villages. Herod's confused identifications (John risen? Elijah? an old prophet?) are the wrong answers to the right question — all of them locate Jesus within the prophetic-restoration tradition rather than recognizing him as its source and fulfillment. His desire to see Jesus (v.9) anticipates the mockery of the Passion, where he finally meets Jesus and is disappointed (23:11).

10.synthesis: The apostles return and report to Jesus what they have done. He withdraws privately to a town called Bethsaida. The crowds learn of it and follow; he welcomes them, speaks to them of the kingdom of God, and heals those in need of healing (vv.10–11). The day begins to wear away; the twelve suggest dismissing the crowd to find provisions. Jesus: "You give them something to eat." They have only five loaves and two fish — unless they are to go and buy food for all these people, about five thousand (vv.12–14). He has them recline in groups of fifty. Taking the five loaves and two fish, he looks up to heaven and blesses and breaks them and gives them to the disciples to set before the crowd. They all eat and are satisfied; twelve baskets of leftovers are taken up (vv.15–17).
10.historical_context: Luke's Feeding of the Five Thousand follows Mark closely but adds that the site is near Bethsaida, a fishing village on the Jordan's entry into the Sea of Galilee. The five loaves and two fish are typical provisions for one person's journey. Five thousand men (the Greek andres specifies males; total including women and children could be significantly higher) reclining in groups of fifty reflects the Exodus wilderness imagery of Israel camped in divisions (Numbers 2). The twelve baskets of leftovers (kophinoi — Jewish travel baskets) correspond to the twelve tribes or the twelve apostles who distributed the food. The fourfold action (took/blessed/broke/gave) is identical to the Last Supper's language, making the feeding a eucharistic anticipation.
10.literary_structure: In Luke the Feeding follows immediately after the Twelve's return from mission — they have just proclaimed the kingdom; now the kingdom is demonstrated in feeding. The crowd in the desert, the shepherd figure providing food, the gathering in groups, the multiplication of bread: all echo Israel in the wilderness fed with manna (Exodus 16) and Elisha feeding a hundred with twenty loaves (2 Kings 4:42–44). The disciples' inadequacy ("we have only five loaves and two fish") is the foil for Jesus's sufficiency — the same pattern as the storm (their panic / his calm) and the mission (their need for provisions / God's provision through hospitality).
10.theological_application: "You give them something to eat" (v.13). The commission to the disciples is deliberately impossible by their own resources — and is the occasion for what Jesus will do through them. This is the paradigm of Christian ministry: the impossible command exposes the inadequacy of our resources and creates the space for Christ's sufficiency to become visible. The disciples distribute the bread they did not multiply — they are instruments, not sources. Their participation is real; the power is not theirs. The twelve baskets remaining demonstrate that the kingdom's provision exceeds the need without remainder: grace is lavish, not calculated.
10.christology: "Taking the five loaves and two fish, he looked up to heaven and said a blessing over them" (v.16). The Eucharistic anticipation (took / gave thanks / broke / gave) identifies Jesus as both the host who provides and the bread that will be broken. John 6:35 makes the identification explicit: "I am the bread of life." The feeding in the wilderness recalls Moses's manna but exceeds it: Moses prayed and the manna came; Jesus blesses and multiplies what is already in hand. He does not wait for divine provision from outside but transforms what is offered. The one who feeds the five thousand is the one who will say "this is my body" in an upper room weeks later — the same eucharistic Lord in two forms of the same gift.

18.synthesis: As Jesus is praying alone with his disciples present, he asks: "Who do the crowds say that I am?" They answer: John the Baptist, Elijah, one of the old prophets risen. "But who do you say that I am?" Peter: "The Christ of God" (vv.18–20). Jesus strictly charges them to tell no one and says the Son of Man must suffer many things — be rejected by elders, chief priests, and scribes — and be killed, and on the third day be raised (vv.21–22). "If anyone would come after me, let him deny himself and take up his cross daily and follow me. For whoever would save his life will lose it, but whoever loses his life for my sake will save it... For the Son of Man is going to come in the glory of his Father with his holy angels" (vv.23–26). Some standing here will not taste death before they see the kingdom of God (v.27).
18.historical_context: Luke frames the confession uniquely: it occurs while Jesus is praying alone (v.18) — the prayer context is characteristically Lukan. "The Christ of God" (ton Christon tou theou) is more explicitly theological than Mark's bare "the Christ" — the Anointed One who belongs to God. Luke omits Matthew's beatitude on Peter and the church-founding saying (Matthew 16:17–19). The "cross daily" (v.23) is a Lukan addition not in Mark/Matthew — the daily dimension extends the cross from the single act of martyrdom to the ongoing discipline of self-renunciation. The saying about some who will not taste death before seeing the kingdom of God (v.27) most plausibly refers to the Transfiguration, which follows immediately.
18.literary_structure: Peter's confession is the structural hinge of all three Synoptic Gospels: the period of public Galilean ministry concludes, and the road to Jerusalem and the Passion begins. In Luke the sequence is: prayer → confession → Passion prediction → cross-discipleship → Transfiguration (v.28 follows immediately). The three elements of the Passion prediction (suffer / be rejected / killed / raised) will be repeated twice more in Luke (9:44; 18:31–33) with increasing specificity. The cross of the disciples (v.23) mirrors the cross of the Master — those who confess him walk the same path.
18.theological_application: "Whoever would save his life will lose it, but whoever loses his life for my sake will save it" (v.24). The double meaning of psychē (life/soul) runs through the saying: the attempt to preserve physical life by avoiding the cross leads to the loss of the true life; the willingness to lose physical life for Christ's sake leads to its true preservation. This is not merely martyrdom teaching (though it includes that) but the fundamental grammar of Christian existence: self-preservation oriented away from Christ is self-destruction; self-surrender to Christ is self-finding. The logic of grain dying to bear fruit (John 12:24) governs the entire saying.
18.christology: "The Christ of God" (v.20) is Peter's confession — and Jesus immediately reinterprets it through the Son of Man's suffering destiny. The Christ whom Peter confesses must suffer, be rejected, die, and rise: being the Christ of God means being the suffering Servant, not the triumphant revolutionary. The crowd's identifications (John, Elijah, old prophets) are all honor titles; Peter's identification is the correct one, but it must be immediately reshaped by the Passion prediction. Christology without the cross is incomplete. The same Son of Man who must suffer will come in the glory of his Father with his holy angels (v.26) — humiliation and exaltation are the two acts of the same drama.

28.synthesis: About eight days after these sayings, Jesus takes Peter, John, and James and goes up on a mountain to pray. As he is praying, the appearance of his face is altered and his clothing becomes dazzling white. Moses and Elijah appear in glory and speak with him about his exodus, which he was about to accomplish at Jerusalem (vv.28–31). Peter and the others are heavy with sleep but stay awake and see his glory and the two men. As Moses and Elijah depart, Peter says, "Master, it is good that we are here. Let us make three tents" — not knowing what he says (vv.32–33). A cloud overshadows them and they are afraid as they enter the cloud. A voice from the cloud: "This is my Son, my Chosen One; listen to him!" When the voice has spoken, Jesus is found alone. They keep silent and tell no one in those days what they saw (vv.34–36).
28.historical_context: Luke's version of the Transfiguration has several unique emphases: (1) it occurs "as he was praying" (v.29); (2) the disciples are "heavy with sleep" and must struggle to stay awake — an anticipation of Gethsemane; (3) most importantly, Luke alone specifies the content of Moses and Elijah's conversation — they spoke about his exodon (exodus, departure) that he was about to fulfill at Jerusalem (v.31). The use of exodos deliberately echoes the Mosaic exodus — Jesus's death-and-resurrection will be the definitive liberation that the Egyptian Exodus prefigured. The cloud is the Shekinah presence of Exodus 40:34–38. The divine address adds "my Chosen One" (ho eklelegmenos) — unique to Luke — combining Psalm 2:7 with Isaiah 42:1 ("my chosen, in whom my soul delights").
28.literary_structure: The Transfiguration follows Peter's Confession as its heavenly confirmation and stands between the first and second Passion predictions as the luminous center of Luke's travel narrative. Moses and Elijah (Law and Prophets) appear to discuss the event that fulfills them both: the exodus Jesus will accomplish. Their disappearance at Peter's suggestion of three tents may imply that Jesus supersedes rather than joins them; the divine voice then confirms it — "listen to him" (autou akouete) echoes Deuteronomy 18:15 ("to him you shall listen"), the prophet-like-Moses text. Jesus is both the fulfillment of Moses's institution and the one to whom Moses directs future attention.
28.theological_application: "This is my Son, my Chosen One; listen to him!" (v.35). The Transfiguration provides the disciples with a glimpse of what Peter has just confessed — the glory of the Christ of God — before the road to the cross makes that confession costly. The mountain-top vision is given precisely when the Passion is beginning to be disclosed, as if God were saying: the one who must suffer is also the Son of glory; the cross you are asked to take up leads to a transfiguration of your own. The three disciples who see the glory are the same three who will sleep in Gethsemane — the glory does not exempt them from the failure, but it remains in their memory as the truth that the Passion cannot finally extinguish.
28.christology: The conversation about the exodos at Jerusalem is the Transfiguration's theological heart: the glory of the Son is not in spite of the cross but through it. Moses and Elijah do not appear to rescue Jesus from his exodus-death but to discuss it — the Law and the Prophets are not surprised by the Passion but have always pointed toward it. The Transfiguration is not an escape from the Passion narrative but the Father's unveiled affirmation of the Son who is going toward it. "My Son, my Chosen One" — the beloved/chosen language of Isaiah 42:1's Servant, here spoken over the dazzlingly white figure on the mountain, shows that the glorified Son and the suffering Servant are the same person in two moments of the same mission.

37.synthesis: The next day, coming down from the mountain, a great crowd meets Jesus. A man cries out: his only son is seized by a spirit that throws him into convulsions with foaming at the mouth and shattering him; the disciples could not cast it out (vv.37–40). Jesus responds with the word "faithless and twisted generation" and calls for the boy; the demon throws him to the ground, but Jesus rebukes the unclean spirit, heals the boy, and gives him back to his father (vv.41–42). While all are marveling at everything he was doing, Jesus says to his disciples: "Let these words sink into your ears: the Son of Man is about to be delivered into the hands of men." They did not understand and its meaning was concealed from them, so that they might not perceive it. They were afraid to ask (vv.43–45).
37.historical_context: The descent from the Transfiguration mountain to this scene creates a stark contrast: the glory of the mountain / the failure in the valley; Moses also descended from Sinai to find Israel's failure (Exodus 32). The medical description — convulsions, foaming, shattering (the verb syntribo — «destroys», unique to Luke's version) — suggests Luke's physician's perspective. That the disciples could not cast it out contrasts with the authority Jesus gave them in 9:1. The concealment saying (v.45: "its meaning was concealed from them, so that they might not perceive it") is unique to Luke — it suggests divine sovereignty in the timing of understanding, not merely human failure. The disciples are preserved from full comprehension of the Passion until after the resurrection, when understanding is opened (24:45).
37.literary_structure: The mountain-to-valley descent is a structural mirror of the Transfiguration: glory → failure, divine affirmation → demonic affliction, Peter's inadequate tent-proposal → the disciples' inadequate exorcism attempt. The insertion of the second Passion prediction immediately after the miraculous healing (while all are marveling) is deliberately jarring: the crowd's amazement at Jesus's power is the very moment he announces the Son of Man's delivery into human hands. The marvel and the Passion are simultaneous realities. Luke's addition "they were afraid to ask" (v.45) shows that the disciples sense enough to be afraid, even if they do not yet understand.
37.theological_application: "The Son of Man is about to be delivered into the hands of men" (v.44). The Aramaic wordplay (bar nasha/bnei nasha — Son of Man delivered into the hands of men/sons of men) is audible in the original. Jesus is the Son of Man who is not delivered by men but gives himself — yet the divine passive (paradidosthai) frames the delivery as something that must happen. The same God who spoke "This is my Son" on the mountain is the one whose plan includes the delivery of that Son into human hands. The Passion is not the defeat of the Transfiguration's glory but the path through which the glory reaches its completion.
37.christology: The healing of the only son — restored and given back to his father (v.42) — is a proleptic image of the resurrection: the Father's only Son will be delivered into human hands and raised on the third day, given back. The Elijah-echo is present (Elijah restored a widow's son and «gave him to his mother», 1 Kings 17:23; Jesus raises Nain's son and «gave him to his mother», 7:15; now he heals this boy and «gave him back to his father» — the parental restoration pattern). But the second Passion prediction immediately following shatters any triumphalist reading: the power to heal and restore is the power that will be handed over. Greatness through service and death is the pattern.

46.synthesis: An argument arises among the disciples about which of them is greatest (v.46). Jesus, knowing the reasoning of their hearts, takes a child and places him beside him: "Whoever receives this child in my name receives me, and whoever receives me receives him who sent me. For he who is least among you all is the one who is great" (vv.47–48). John reports they saw someone casting out demons in Jesus's name and tried to stop him; Jesus: "Do not stop him, for the one who is not against you is for you" (vv.49–50). When the days drew near for Jesus to be taken up, he set his face to go to Jerusalem (v.51). A Samaritan village does not receive him because his face is set toward Jerusalem; James and John want fire from heaven; Jesus rebukes them. They go on to another village (vv.52–56).
46.historical_context: The children-in-midst episode is placed differently in each Synoptic. Luke places it immediately after the second Passion prediction and before the travel narrative — the greatness dispute and the Passion prediction are juxtaposed: while Jesus is describing his humiliation and death, the disciples argue about rank. The Samaritan rejection introduces the Journey narrative proper: Jesus has "set his face" (a Hebrew idiom from Isaiah 50:7 — the Servant's face set like flint toward suffering) and the Samaritans perceive the direction and close their villages to a pilgrim heading for Jerusalem. James and John's request for fire from heaven echoes Elijah calling down fire (2 Kings 1:10–12) — an Elijah-comparison the disciples have been primed to make by the Transfiguration, which they now misapply.
46.literary_structure: Luke 9:51 is one of the most significant structural verses in the Gospel: "When the days drew near for him to be taken up, he set his face to go to Jerusalem." It opens the Travel Narrative (9:51–19:27) — the longest section of Luke, with no parallel in Mark/Matthew, dominated by unique Lukan material (parables of the Good Samaritan, Prodigal Son, Rich Man and Lazarus, etc.). The "taken up" (analēmpsis) foreshadows both the Ascension of Acts 1 and the Passion — Luke frames the journey as oriented toward the whole of the Christ event, not merely the cross. The child placed in the midst is the kingdom's visual argument against the disciples' greatness-logic: service to the least is service to Christ and to the Father.
46.theological_application: "He who is least among you all is the one who is great" (v.48). The child represents those who have no social standing, no claim to honor, and no ability to reciprocate hospitality — receiving such a person «in Jesus's name» is the kingdom's definition of greatness. The disciples' argument about greatness is the perpetual temptation of religious communities to transpose the world's honor-and-status logic into the church. Jesus's answer is not to establish a revised hierarchy (the most humble is secretly the greatest) but to point to the child as the measure: the kingdom is not about position but about presence — who you welcome, not where you rank.
46.christology: "He set his face to go to Jerusalem" (v.51). The Servant of Isaiah 50:7 set his face like flint because the Sovereign LORD would vindicate him — he went toward suffering with the confidence of the one who knows the Father's faithfulness. Jesus's Jerusalem-orientation is not resignation or fate but resolved obedience: he is going to his exodos, the departure that Moses and Elijah discussed on the mountain. The fire that James and John want to call down is the wrong kind of power — retributive judgment on those who reject the king. Jesus does not command fire on the Samaritan village but continues the journey toward the place where he himself will be consumed by the fire of the Father's judgment on behalf of all who reject him.

57.synthesis: As they travel along the road, a man says to Jesus, "I will follow you wherever you go." Jesus: "Foxes have holes, and birds of the air have nests, but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head" (vv.57–58). To another he says, "Follow me." The man: "Lord, let me first go and bury my father." Jesus: "Leave the dead to bury their own dead. But as for you, go and proclaim the kingdom of God" (vv.59–60). Yet another: "I will follow you, Lord, but let me first say farewell to those at my home." Jesus: "No one who puts his hand to the plow and looks back is fit for the kingdom of God" (vv.61–62). Three would-be disciples, three obstacles, three answers — each clarifying the unconditional claim that following Jesus on the Jerusalem road makes.
57.historical_context: The three sayings occur at the beginning of the Travel Narrative, shaping what it means to join the Jerusalem-bound company. The Son of Man's homelessness (v.58) is historically plausible: the itinerant ministry had no fixed base since leaving Capernaum. "Let me first bury my father" was one of the highest filial obligations in Jewish culture — a son was expected to remain with an aged father until his death and burial; "my father has died" or "is dying soon" is the likely sense. Jesus's response is not callousness toward death but the eschatological urgency of the kingdom: the proclamation cannot wait. The Elisha echo is present (Elijah called Elisha who asked to kiss his parents goodbye — 1 Kings 19:19–21; Jesus's call is more demanding than Elijah's).
57.literary_structure: The three encounter-sayings function as a triptych of discipleship costs: home (the Son of Man has no home; will you follow one who is homeless?), family obligation (will kingdom proclamation displace the highest family duty?), and looking back (Elisha slaughtered his oxen and burned his plow — no turning back; Lot's wife looked back — Genesis 19:26). Together they address the three primary attachments that compete with total discipleship: security, family, and the past. They appear at the opening of the Travel Narrative as its threshold conditions: to go to Jerusalem with Jesus is to face exactly these choices at escalating intensity.
57.theological_application: "No one who puts his hand to the plow and looks back is fit for the kingdom of God" (v.62). The plow-metaphor requires forward vision: a plowman who looks backward plows a crooked furrow. Kingdom work requires the same orientation — the disciple who has set out for Jerusalem must not keep their heart in the village behind. This does not mean severing all past relationships but that the Jerusalem direction must be primary. The three obstacles (homelessness anxiety, family obligation, nostalgic attachment) are not sins — they are reasonable human goods that become idols when they prevent the radical following that the road to the cross requires.
57.christology: "The Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head" (v.58). The one who asks others to follow him into homelessness has himself accepted homelessness as the form of his mission. The Creator who made every nest and burrow has in his incarnation no resting place of his own — the foxes and birds have what the Son of Man has surrendered. The Travel Narrative is a sustained journey toward a city that will kill him; the homelessness of the road is the shape of the mission. Those who follow him will share this homelessness — "we have no lasting city but we seek the city that is to come" (Hebrews 13:14) — and will find in his resurrection the first ground of the new home he is preparing.
